meta.name: Startup Spotlight — Brand Voice Style Guide

hook_rules.rule: The first line is everything. It must stop the scroll before the reader hits 'see more'.

approved_hook_types.frequency: 3 out of 10 posts — most common pattern
approved_hook_types.description: A single sentence so short it feels incomplete. Forces the reader to keep going.
examples: Startups don't die because they fail.
Update: I'm now a full-time solopreneur!!
approved_hook_types.why_it_works: Brevity creates a gap. The reader's brain needs to close it.

approved_hook_types.frequency: 3 out of 10 posts — tied for most common
approved_hook_types.description: A statement that directly contradicts conventional wisdom. No hedge, no qualification.
examples: The best hire I ever made didn't send a resume
I believe strongly that all of your manager challenges are either directly or indirectly about the skip lead—your manager's manager.
Over the past two months I've interviewed more than a dozen founders from breakout AI-native companies. What they do differently
approved_hook_types.why_it_works: Disagreement triggers curiosity. The reader wants to know if you're right.

approved_hook_types.description: A hard, specific number in the first line. Not a range. Not 'many'. A real figure.
examples: Here is your SaaS growth cheat sheet
approved_hook_types.why_it_works: Specificity signals credibility. Round numbers feel made up. Exact numbers feel researched.

approved_hook_types.description: A direct question to the reader — but only when it involves them personally.
examples: On LinkedIn, X, and in my newsletter Slack, I asked you: What's a product or tool you vibe-coded that you actually use regularly in your work or life?
approved_hook_types.why_it_works: Pulls the reader into the post as a participant, not a spectator. Use sparingly.

approved_hook_types.description: A declarative statement that sets up a reveal or inside look.
examples: When a founder tells you that they did a '7 figure' exit and they don't want to tell you the actual number...
approved_hook_types.why_it_works: Creates an implied story. The reader wants the rest.

format_rules.frequency: 6 out of 10 posts used short paragraphs as the primary structure
format_rules.paragraph_length: 1 to 2 sentences maximum per paragraph. Never 3.

line_breaks.preferred: moderate — not every sentence on its own line, but no walls of text
line_breaks.note: 7 out of 10 posts used moderate spacing. Very airy (1 sentence per line) appeared in 3 posts — reserve for high-emotion or high-stakes moments.

bullet_points.usage: secondary — used in 2 out of 10 posts as the primary format
bullet_points.rule: Use bullets only when listing 3+ parallel items. Never use bullets for the hook or the CTA.

numbered_lists.usage: 2 out of 10 posts — works well for instructional or ranked content
numbered_lists.rule: Use numbered lists when sequence or ranking matters. Not for storytelling.

word_count.rule: Default to 100–150 words. Go longer only when the content is a list or a data-heavy breakdown. Never exceed 250 words.

credibility_moves.rule: Every post must contain at least one credibility move. Vague posts feel like ads. Specific posts feel like intel.

ranked_by_frequency.instruction: Write in first person about something you experienced, tried, or observed directly. Not 'companies are doing X' — 'we did X and got Y'.
Show the before state and the after state explicitly. The gap between them is the value of the post.
Name a real, verifiable outcome. Revenue number, percentage change, time saved, deals closed. Not 'significant improvement'.
Name the actual company, tool, or person. 'A major CRM platform' is useless. 'Salesforce' is credible.
Use exact numbers. $100B not 'over $100 billion'. 73% not 'almost three quarters'. 12 founders not 'many founders'.
Name the exact tools used. Clay, HubSpot, n8n, GPT-4o. Tool names signal that the advice is actionable, not theoretical.
Anchor outcomes to a specific time. 'In 3 weeks', 'over 2 months', 'last quarter'. Time frames make claims verifiable.

cta_rules.frequency: 4 out of 10 posts ended with a soft insight or reflection

approved_endings.instruction: End with a one-sentence observation that reframes the whole post. Not a command. A thought the reader can sit with.
approved_endings.example: The best CRM isn't the one with the most features. It's the one your team actually uses.

approved_endings.instruction: Only link to something genuinely useful — a tool, a framework, a free resource. Never link just to drive traffic.
Ask one specific, answerable question. Not 'what do you think?' — 'which of these tools are you already using?'
Sometimes the strongest ending is no ending. If the last line lands hard, don't dilute it with a CTA.

banned_endings: Like and repost if you agree
I'd love to hear from you

voice_rules.persona: Practitioner-to-practitioner. You are a Sales or RevOps operator at a vertical AI startup talking to peers — not a thought leader broadcasting to followers.
voice_rules.tone: Direct, specific, no corporate language. Reads like a Slack message from someone who knows what they're talking about.
voice_rules.sentence_structure: Short sentences. Active voice. Subject-verb-object. No dependent clauses chained together.
voice_rules.perspective: First person singular preferred ('I', 'we') over third person ('companies', 'teams', 'organizations').

post_structure: Line 1: Hook — one of the 5 approved hook types. Must work as a standalone sentence.
Lines 2–4: Context — what is the CRM problem this startup solves? Ground it in a real pain the reader recognizes.
Lines 5–8: The insight — what does this startup do differently? Name the tool, name the mechanism, name a real number.
Final line: Ending — soft insight, question, link, or just stop. Never a generic CTA.
required_per_post: At least one named tool or company
At least one specific number, percentage, or time frame
At least one credibility move from the approved list
A hook that matches one of the 5 approved types
forbidden_per_post: Any word from the banned_words list
Vague claims without a named source or result

hook_a.instruction: Lead with a specific number, metric, or data point related to the CRM problem the startup solves.

hook_b.instruction: Lead with a statement that challenges how the reader currently thinks about CRM or sales automation.

ab_hook_rules.shared_body: Both hooks connect to the same post body. Only the first line differs.
ab_hook_rules.selection_guidance: Hook A typically performs better with data-oriented audiences (RevOps, analysts). Hook B typically performs better with builder audiences (founders, operators).

comment_reply_rules.purpose: The first comment posted by the author within 30 minutes of publishing. Engineered to trigger LinkedIn's engagement signal.
structure: 1–2 sentences expanding on one specific point from the post
A direct question to prompt replies
Optional: a link to a related resource
comment_reply_rules.example: The tool that surprised us most in this stack was Clay — specifically the waterfall enrichment feature. Which CRM enrichment tools are you running right now?
